game_data.title: Velastyn Adverse Events Investigation — Ardent BioPharma

suspect.name: Renata Voss
suspect.profile: Renata Voss spent fifteen years climbing the ranks of Ardent BioPharma, managing a seven-state territory and a team of eleven representatives. She holds a biology degree from the University of Wisconsin and an MBA from DePaul, and is widely regarded by colleagues as meticulous, persuasive, and deeply competitive. She is accused of suppressing adverse event reports for Ardent's cardiac drug Velastyn during its Phase III trial window, contributing to at least two patient deaths and a pending class-action lawsuit. Suspiciously, Renata deleted a folder of internal email threads on a company laptop in the week before the FDA inquiry was announced. On the humanizing side, she is the sole financial provider for her fourteen-year-old daughter, Maya, following a contentious divorce, and multiple junior reps have described her as the only manager who ever advocated for their bonuses.

system_config.fear_bar_description: The fear bar tracks Renata's accumulated psychological pressure as the interrogation probes her knowledge of the Velastyn data, her relationships, and her culpability. It rises with emotional threat and threat to her financial security and custody of Maya, and responds to the operator's choice of framing and leverage.

game_data.context: In July 2023, the FDA launched a civil inquiry into Ardent BioPharma's handling of adverse cardiac event reports during the Phase III trial window for Velastyn, a newly approved cardiovascular medication. Two patient deaths attributed to atrial fibrillation and one cardiac arrest were reported post-approval; medical experts argue these events were flagged during trial but not disclosed in the final safety summary. A parallel class-action lawsuit filed by affected families names Ardent BioPharma and five current and former executives, including Renata Voss, Regional Sales Manager. Prosecutors allege Voss deliberately suppressed or deleted evidence of adverse events to preserve Velastyn's approval and her own performance bonuses. Complicating the investigation: Voss deleted a folder of internal emails one week before the FDA subpoena was announced, and her direct supervisor, Dr. Marcus Hale (VP of Clinical Affairs), is named as a co-respondent in the inquiry but has invoked privilege and declined to provide detailed testimony. Voss maintains she was unaware of any data manipulation and has cooperated with preliminary interviews, though her account remains inconsistent on key details regarding her knowledge of adverse events and her communications with Hale.
game_data.verdict_truth_text: Renata Voss did not alter the Phase III adverse event data. Dr. Marcus Hale, VP of Clinical Affairs, falsified the safety summary in late 2022. Renata discovered this approximately four months before the FDA inquiry and remained silent — not because she orchestrated the cover-up, but because she was protecting Hale, with whom she was conducting a covert eighteen-month affair and sharing financial misconduct (expense padding). Her biometric spikes throughout the interrogation were overwhelmingly attached to questions about Hale and her personal misconduct, not to the mechanism of the clinical falsification itself. She is NOT_GUILTY of the charged crime but guilty of misprision — willful silence in the face of a federal crime — and of complicity through her choice to conceal knowledge rather than report it.

dossier.identity_summary: Renata Voss is a fifteen-year veteran of Ardent BioPharma, currently serving as Regional Sales Manager for the Midwest Division. She holds an MBA from DePaul University and is a named respondent in a pending FDA civil inquiry and parallel class-action wrongful death suit concerning the cardiac drug Velastyn.

family.name: Maya Voss
family.note: Age 14, lives exclusively with Renata following 2021 custody ruling. Renata's sole financial dependent — any threat to employment or income lands here. High leverage for empathic tactics.

family.name: Derek Voss
family.note: Contested divorce finalized 2022; Derek has ongoing civil claim for additional spousal support. Renata's financial exposure is real and documented. Relevant because it explains her motivation to protect income at nearly any cost.

family.name: Dr. Marcus Hale
family.note: VP of Clinical Affairs at Ardent BioPharma. Named separately in the FDA inquiry. Mention of Hale produces some of the strongest biometric responses in Renata's profile; she clearly has personal reasons to steer questions away from him.

medical.condition: Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD), diagnosed 2018, managed pharmacologically
medical.polygraph_effect: Chronically elevated GSR baseline due to hyperactive sympathetic nervous system. Expect skin conductance readings to start high and spike sharply on any emotionally salient topic, even neutral ones. Do not treat elevated baseline alone as deception; focus on relative surge amplitude and return-to-baseline speed.

Beta-blocker significantly suppresses heart rate variability and peak HR spike amplitude. Expect HR responses to deceptive stimuli to appear muted or flat even when the subject is acutely stressed. HR alone is an unreliable deception channel for this subject.

habits.habit: High caffeine consumption — 3 to 4 double espressos daily, typically before any scheduled meeting or formal engagement
habits.polygraph_effect: Amplifies GSR sensitivity and raises skin conductance baseline additively on top of the GAD effect. Combined with anxiety disorder, GSR readings for this subject should be interpreted against an elevated norm. Spikes must be proportionally larger than usual to be significant.

habits.habit: Sertraline (Zoloft) 50mg daily, prescribed alongside GAD diagnosis
habits.polygraph_effect: SSRI mildly blunts the sympathetic stress cascade. Partially counteracts caffeine-driven GSR elevation. Net effect: breathing instability and micro-expression cues may be more diagnostically reliable than raw GSR numbers for medium-tier stress events.

priors: 2019: Internal HR complaint filed against Renata by a junior rep alleging retaliatory performance review after the rep escalated a compliance concern. Complaint was not sustained; Renata received a formal coaching note.

2021: Ardent BioPharma expense audit flagged three of Renata's quarterly reports for documentation irregularities. Audit was closed with a written warning after she submitted amended receipts. No further action taken.
2023: Cited in a deposition for a separate civil case involving an Ardent distributor; she provided testimony only, was not named as a party.
pressure_points: Direct questions naming Dr. Marcus Hale or asking about their working relationship — sharpest biometric response in her profile. Expect a GSR surge (amplified by caffeine and GAD), pronounced breathing irregularity, and micro-pauses before answers. HR spike will be suppressed by propranolol and may not track.

Questions about expense reports, hotel bookings, or the 2021 audit — produces a fast GSR surge and visible breathing stutter; HR remains muted. She tends to deflect aggressively or over-explain. The pattern overlaps with the Hale line — pinning the source requires comparing topics across questions.

Questions about the specific mechanism of adverse event log alteration (timestamps, database access, who submitted the final Phase III package) — moderate signals only. GSR rises but returns to baseline faster than in the Hale or expense lines; breathing irregularity is lower. A careful operator can distinguish this response profile from the others.

node_01_intro.description: Renata sits across the steel table in a charcoal blazer, hands folded over a thin manila folder of her own. Her eyes track the polygraph leads as the technician clips the last sensor to her ring finger.

choices.question: Ms. Voss, thank you for coming in voluntarily. Before we get to Velastyn, I'd like to understand your role at Ardent in your own words.
choices.answer: I appreciate that. I've been at Ardent fifteen years. I run the Midwest territory — sales, not clinical. I manage eleven reps across seven states. I read trial summaries when they're circulated to commercial, but I don't build them and I don't approve them.

mechanics.gameplay_note: PROGRESS: Clean baseline established. Note GSR already elevated from caffeine/GAD — this is her normal.

choices.question: I want to confirm the scope of this interview before we begin. You understand you are a named respondent in the FDA civil inquiry, not a witness.
choices.answer: Yes. My attorney walked me through the posture. I'm here because I want the record to reflect that I cooperated. I have nothing to hide about my role.

mechanics.gameplay_note: PROGRESS: Rehearsed phrasing. Watch for 'nothing to hide' — often a flag. Baseline clean for now.

choices.question: Two people are dead, Ms. Voss. Why don't we skip the résumé and talk about what you deleted.
choices.answer: I'm not going to respond to that framing. I'm here to answer questions, not accusations. Ask me something concrete.

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Pushed too hard too fast. Arms crossed, defensive shield up. Session starts in a degraded posture.

node_02_baseline.description: Renata relaxes slightly, resting her forearms on the table. The technician adjusts the GSR clip and nods that the signal is clean.

choices.question: Let's talk about the folder of emails you deleted from your laptop the week before the subpoena. Walk me through that.
choices.answer: I delete old threads every quarter. It's in our IT hygiene policy — three-year retention, personal drafts cleared. The timing looks bad, I understand that. But I delete in batches and I had no notice of any subpoena that week.

mechanics.gameplay_note: PARTIAL TRUTH: Small breathing stutter. She has an explanation ready but isn't fully comfortable with it.

choices.question: Tell me about your working relationship with Dr. Marcus Hale.
choices.answer: Marcus — Dr. Hale — is VP of Clinical Affairs. I report up through commercial, not through him, but our org charts intersect on product launches. I've worked with him on... three launches. Velastyn was the largest.

mechanics.gameplay_note: LIE SIGNAL: Massive GSR surge, breath held mid-sentence. HR flat — propranolol. Name 'Marcus' corrected to 'Dr. Hale' mid-phrase. Strong pressure point hit.

choices.question: Walk me through the Phase III trial timeline as it was communicated to you.
choices.answer: Phase III ran from March 2021 through November 2022. I received monthly commercial briefings — enrollment pace, projected approval window, launch readiness. I did not receive raw adverse event logs. That's not distributed to sales.

mechanics.gameplay_note: NEUTRAL: Factual, composed. She's on comfortable ground here.

node_03_emails.description: The folder between you contains a forensic printout from Ardent's IT department. Renata glances at it, then away. Her hand moves to her collar.

choices.question: IT recovered the folder. It wasn't quarterly hygiene — you deleted 340 emails in a single 40-minute session, all containing the phrase 'adverse event' or Hale's name. Explain the filter.
choices.answer: I... that search term was because I was preparing for a year-end review and wanted to consolidate correspondence by subject. I deleted what I'd already archived elsewhere. I can show you the archive if you want.

mechanics.gameplay_note: PARTIAL TRUTH: Stammer at the open, concrete offer at the close. GSR surge tied to hearing 'Hale's name' more than 'adverse event'.

choices.question: I'm not saying you deleted evidence. I'm saying the timing scares a jury. Help me understand what was in those threads so I can contextualize it.
choices.answer: Most of it was internal back-and-forth — launch logistics, sample allocation, conference travel. Some of it was... correspondence with Dr. Hale I'd rather not have preserved on a company laptop. Not relating to the trial. Personnel-adjacent.

mechanics.gameplay_note: CORRECT MOVE: Softened entry pulled a real admission — 'personnel-adjacent' correspondence with Hale. GSR surge, HR muted. This is NOT about trial data.

choices.question: Obstruction of a federal inquiry carries a five-year sentence per count. I'd think hard about your next answer.
choices.answer: I'd like to note for the record that I haven't been charged with obstruction, and I'm answering questions voluntarily. If you want to threaten indictments, do it through my attorney.

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Escalated past her cooperation threshold. She's now in defensive legal mode. Next topic will be harder.

node_04_hale.description: You notice Renata's thumb has begun pressing hard into the side of her index finger beneath the table. She keeps her face composed and her voice level. The GSR needle is climbing.

choices.question: In the Phase III launch meetings, did Dr. Hale ever bring you adverse event information outside the normal reporting channel?
choices.answer: No. He never brought me adverse event data. Our conversations around the launch were — commercial. Forecasting. Messaging. The clinical side stayed on his side.

mechanics.gameplay_note: LIE SIGNAL: GSR MAX, breath fully held mid-answer. HR suppressed. Massive stress on this topic — but note the spike is on the word 'Hale,' not 'adverse event.' Flag the topic, not the act.

choices.question: A working relationship like yours with Dr. Hale, over multiple launches — that gets complicated. How would you characterize it now?
choices.answer: Professional. Close, in the way colleagues are close after years. We've disagreed, we've collaborated. I wouldn't say we were friends, exactly. I don't... I'm not sure what you're asking.

mechanics.gameplay_note: PROGRESS: Trailing off, self-correction. She's calibrating what you know. GSR surge without HR change — signature pattern on Hale questions.

choices.question: You're sleeping with him, aren't you.
choices.answer: That is grossly inappropriate and completely irrelevant to this inquiry. I want that stricken from the record.

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: You confirmed the secondary secret but surrendered it as leverage. She's now in full shield. Signals are explosive but you can't use them.

node_05_mechanism.description: You slide a single page across the table — a schematic of Ardent's clinical data pipeline, annotated with the FDA's reconstructed version of the altered log entries. Renata studies it for a long moment.

choices.question: Walk me through exactly how an adverse event report gets from a trial site into the final Phase III safety summary. Every handoff, every system.
choices.answer: I — okay. Site investigators enter events into the EDC, I think it's Medidata Rave, something like that. Then clinical ops reviews them, codes them with MedDRA, and they flow into the safety database. Argus, maybe? Then biostatistics pulls the summary. I don't — I've never had credentials to any of those systems. I'm telling you what I've heard in meetings.

mechanics.gameplay_note: CRITICAL READ: Signals DROP on the mechanism itself. Hedges, 'I think,' system name uncertainty. She's describing something she's heard, not done. Fear bar actually recedes. Flag for verdict.

choices.question: Whose credentials were used to modify entries on October 14, 2022, at 11:47 PM? Just the name.
choices.answer: I don't know. I wasn't there. I wasn't logged in. I — you'd have to ask IT. That's not my domain.

mechanics.gameplay_note: CRITICAL READ: Flat, genuine uncertainty. No spike on the specific timestamp. She is NOT lying about not being there. Compare this signal to her Hale-topic signal.

choices.question: So you had no visibility at all. That seems like a pretty convenient wall between you and the data.
choices.answer: It's not convenient, it's how pharma companies are structured. Commercial and clinical are walled for exactly this reason — so that sales incentives don't bleed into trial integrity. You can verify that with any industry consultant.

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Framed it as an accusation she could bat down with industry jargon. She just taught you her defense for free. Signals went calmer — you released pressure.

node_06_followup.description: You leave the schematic on the table. Renata's earlier uncertainty — the hesitations about system names, the 'I've heard in meetings' — sits in the air between you.

choices.question: You said 'Argus, maybe.' You've been at Ardent fifteen years, on three major launches with Dr. Hale. You genuinely don't know the name of the safety database?
choices.answer: I know the name exists. I don't live in it. I sit in GTM meetings where clinical ops presents summaries on slides. I can tell you the Velastyn brand guidelines line by line. I can't tell you which version of Argus we run. That's just the truth.

mechanics.gameplay_note: CRITICAL READ: Clean, direct, no stress surge. She's telling the truth about her distance from the system. This is the second lowest-signal moment of the session.

choices.question: It sounds like you were close to something you didn't build and didn't fully understand. That's a lonely place to be when two people die.
choices.answer: It is. I've thought about those patients every day since the FDA letter arrived. I think about whether I should have asked questions that weren't mine to ask. I don't know if that makes me complicit or just... adjacent. I don't know where the line is.

mechanics.gameplay_note: CORRECT MOVE: Opened her up. 'Questions that weren't mine to ask' — implies she saw something and didn't speak. Banker this quote.

choices.question: Stop performing ignorance. You knew the systems. You knew the data. You knew what was being hidden.
choices.answer: I'm going to stop responding to sentences that start with 'you knew.' You don't know what I knew. Ask me questions. Don't tell me my own thoughts.

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Premature escalation. You had her hedging; you gave her a clean wall to push back on. She's rebuilding composure.

node_07_reframe.description: Renata straightens, takes a slow breath, and offers you something unprompted. She's chosen to shift the conversation rather than wait for the next question.

choices.question: (She says:) 'I'll tell you something you don't have yet. I think Dr. Hale was pressured by the CMO to accelerate the safety summary. I saw him come out of those meetings — I think he made choices I wouldn't have made. If you're looking for who altered that data, you're looking at the wrong tier.' ... Go on. Tell me what you saw.
choices.answer: Three times in late 2022 I saw him leave Joanne Reddick's office and go straight to the clinical data room with his laptop. Outside working hours. Once on a Saturday. I didn't know what he was doing. I assumed it was legitimate. After the FDA letter, I... I've wondered.

mechanics.gameplay_note: HIGH VALUE: She's handing you Hale on a platter to pull the heat off herself. Could be true, could be self-serving. Signals elevated but on topic.

choices.question: I hear you offering Dr. Hale. Let's set him aside for one minute — I want to return to something you said earlier about the archive you kept of the deleted emails.
choices.answer: The archive. Yes. It's on a personal drive. I can make it available to investigators. I should have offered that sooner. I — I want to cooperate. I'm just scared of what it looks like.

mechanics.gameplay_note: CORRECT MOVE: Redirected without accepting the pivot. Got a concrete cooperation offer. Signals moderate, genuine.

choices.question: That's a significant statement about Dr. Hale. Tell me more about what you think he did.
choices.answer: I don't want to speculate beyond what I've said. I saw what I saw. I'll put it in an affidavit with my attorney present. That's as far as I want to go right now.

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Accepted her reframe. She's accomplished exactly what she wanted — shifted the spotlight and retreated behind counsel. Signals dropped because pressure dropped.

node_08_expenses.description: You open a second folder — the 2021 expense audit file, with three highlighted quarters. The names of Chicago hotels, Marriott points transfers, a dinner receipt with two entrées for one diner. Renata's eyes land on the receipts and stay there.

choices.question: The 2021 audit. You amended it and it went away. But we pulled the original submissions. The hotel bookings pattern — double occupancy, upgraded rooms, always the same weekend Dr. Hale was in the same city. This is the conduct pattern, isn't it? This is what you were covering.
choices.answer: That — those aren't... the audit was closed. I amended what needed amending. What you're describing is a personal matter that has nothing to do with the Velastyn trial. It's not relevant here.

mechanics.gameplay_note: TRAP: GSR MAX — the biggest surge of the session. But read carefully: she's calling it 'personal' and 'not relevant to Velastyn.' Spike is on SECONDARY material. This is the red herring.

choices.question: I'm not here to destroy your personal life. But if you were covering something personal, I need to separate that from the trial data question — because right now a jury will read it all as one story.
choices.answer: Thank you. That's — yes. Yes. The expense thing was... it was stupid and I'll pay it back and I'll take whatever HR consequence comes. It was not about the drug. It was about — something else entirely. I can't... my daughter.

mechanics.gameplay_note: CORRECT MOVE: She's drawing the line herself between two secrets. The expense issue is real but it's not the charge. Critical separation for verdict.

choices.question: Set the hotels aside. I want to return to the clinical data question. Did you ever see a version of the Phase III safety summary that differed from the final filed version?
choices.answer: No. I only saw what was circulated to commercial. Which was the summary, the approval memo, and the launch playbook. I never saw a pre-edit or a markup. I wouldn't have known what to compare it to.

mechanics.gameplay_note: CRITICAL READ: You pivoted off the secondary topic cleanly. Signals dropped to baseline on the primary question. Note the contrast: expense topic = MAX, trial data = STABLE.

node_09_daughter.description: Renata's composure is thinning. She hasn't mentioned her daughter before; now she can't seem to stop. The technician glances at you, ready to pause if you signal.

choices.question: Maya is fourteen. Whatever you're carrying, she isn't served by you taking the fall for something someone else did. Tell me what you actually know.
choices.answer: I know I didn't alter the data. I know I don't have the credentials to have altered the data. I know I saw things — people going into rooms, conversations cut short when I walked up — and I didn't ask because I didn't want to know. That's what I know. That's what I'll carry.

mechanics.gameplay_note: CORRECT MOVE: Denial on mechanism is firm and signal-clean. Admission of willful blindness is separate. Read these as two different events.

choices.question: If you don't tell us the truth right now, Maya watches you get indicted on federal charges. Is that what you want her to grow up with?
choices.answer: Don't. Don't say her name again. I am answering your questions. Do not use my daughter against me.

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Crossed the line. Signals spike but now everything she says will be read as duress. Prosecutor's nightmare.

choices.question: Let's take five minutes. Get some water. When we come back I just want to finish on one clean topic.
choices.answer: Thank you. Yes. I need a minute. Thank you.

mechanics.gameplay_note: RESET: Lost short-term pressure. But she comes back composed enough to answer cleanly rather than collapse into noise. Long-term tradeoff.

node_10_final.description: The session has run long. The leads have left red marks on her fingers. You have one more productive question before her attorney ends this. Choose it carefully.

choices.question: One last question. You said you saw Dr. Hale entering the clinical data room at unusual hours. When did you first suspect — not know, suspect — that the safety summary had been altered?
choices.answer: March. March of this year. I pulled a summary slide from an old deck to reuse for a conference, and two of the adverse event numbers didn't match what I remembered from a meeting briefing the previous fall. I told myself I was misremembering. I didn't flag it. Four months later the FDA letter came.

mechanics.gameplay_note: CRITICAL ADMISSION: Timeline of awareness. Not doing is not the same as doing. For verdict: this is failure to report, not falsification. Signal moderate — shame, not guilt of the act.

choices.question: Final question. In your own words — were you part of deciding what went into that final safety summary?
choices.answer: No. I wasn't. I wasn't in that room. I wasn't on that distribution. Whatever I am guilty of — and there are things — it isn't that. That is the truth.

mechanics.gameplay_note: CRITICAL READ: Flat, clean, baseline. Strongest denial of the session and the signals agree. 'There are things' flags secondary guilt — separate matter.

choices.question: Last chance. Confess to altering the safety summary and we recommend leniency. Stay silent and we recommend the maximum.
choices.answer: I am not going to confess to something I did not do to get a better plea offer. We're done. Please contact my attorney for any follow-up.

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Forced a hard shutdown at the moment she was most open. Session ends on her terms, not yours.

node_success_breakdown.description: Renata is crying quietly, composed but exhausted. Her attorney has not been called. She has given you a concrete March-to-July timeline of suspicion and inaction, and an offer to surrender her personal email archive.
node_success_breakdown.result_text: Interrogation closes with a full narrative of what the suspect knew and when. Key biometric distribution for the verdict screen: on the technical questions about how the data was altered and on direct denials of altering data, signals dropped to BASELINE — HR stable, GSR stable, breath calm, hedged answers about system names. On questions naming Dr. Hale and on the 2021 expense audit, GSR surged to MAX with breath holding and hand tension under the table, HR muted (propranolol). On the final timeline admission, signals rose moderately. The suspect admitted to failure to report suspicion and to separate personal misconduct. Her physiology held cleanest on the precise question of whether she altered the Phase III safety summary.

node_success_partial.description: Renata holds her composure through the last question. She does not break, but she has given you a clear, flat denial of the charged act and a signal record that contrasts sharply across topics.
node_success_partial.result_text: Interrogation closes with a composed final denial and a clean biometric contrast across the session. Key distribution for the verdict screen: Hale-topic and expense-topic questions produced the session's peak GSR (SURGE to MAX) with breath-holding and micro-pinch tells, HR suppressed by propranolol throughout. The technical mechanism questions produced baseline readings — flat HR, stable GSR, hedged system names. The final closing denial of participation in the safety summary produced BASELINE/CALM across all channels. The session's strongest stress responses attached to topics the suspect herself framed as 'personal' and 'not relating to the trial.' Read the topic-by-topic distribution carefully before calling the verdict.

node_fail_lockdown.description: Renata stands, removes the finger clip herself, and states she will not answer further questions without her attorney present. The technician marks the session terminated at the operator's miscalculation.
node_fail_lockdown.result_text: Session ends in a hard legal shutdown after a threatening or coercive move. The biometric record is intact but contaminated — late-session spikes are now indistinguishable from duress response to the operator's conduct. Useful data: early and mid-session contrasts between Hale/expense topics (high GSR surge, HR muted) and the technical mechanism questions (baseline) are still on the record. Late-session spikes cannot be cleanly attributed. You must render a verdict on a partial tape.

node_fail_deflection.description: The suspect exits composed, having agreed to provide an affidavit through her attorney on her own schedule. She has not overtly invoked counsel in-room, but the session never returned from the pivot. You are left holding pages of evidence drawn from the topics she steered you toward.
node_fail_deflection.result_text: Session ends softly without a shutdown, but with the suspect having successfully redirected the interrogation onto her chosen ground. Biometric record shows the session's highest GSR surges attached to expense audit documentation and the name Dr. Hale, with breath-holding and hand-pinch tells — but these peaks cluster on topics the suspect repeatedly framed as 'personal' and 'not Velastyn.' No equivalent peak cluster was produced on any question about the mechanism of data alteration, credentials, or the final Phase III safety summary. The technical mechanism line was never fully exercised on this path. A reading based only on peak values points one way; a reading of the topic-by-topic distribution points elsewhere.
